title: Classification
description: Comprehensive quiz bank for Module 2: Classification covering all 6 sessions

sessions.title: Introduction to Classification & First Classifier (Iris)
sessions.description: Covers classification basics, Iris dataset, train/test split, and first model training

questions.question: What is the main difference between classification and regression?
options: Classification predicts categories, regression predicts continuous values
Classification is harder than regression
Regression uses neural networks, classification doesn't
They are the same thing
questions.explanation: Classification predicts discrete categories (spam/not spam, cat/dog, iris species), while regression predicts continuous numerical values (price, temperature, stock price).

questions.question: In the Iris dataset, what does the term 'features' refer to?
options: The species names (setosa, versicolor, virginica)
The measurements (sepal length, sepal width, petal length, petal width)
The number of flowers in the dataset
The accuracy of the model
questions.explanation: Features (also called inputs or X) are the measurements used to make predictions. In Iris, these are the 4 flower measurements. The species names are the labels/targets (y).

questions.question: Why do we split data into training and testing sets?
options: To make the dataset smaller and faster to process
To evaluate model performance on unseen data
Because scikit-learn requires it
To create more data for training
questions.explanation: We split data to test the model on data it has never seen before. Testing on training data would be like studying with the exact exam questions - you'd get perfect scores but wouldn't know if you truly learned. The test set simulates real-world use.

questions.question: What does the model.fit(X_train, y_train) method do?
options: Tests the model on test data
Trains/teaches the model using training data
Makes predictions on new data
Deletes the model from memory
questions.explanation: fit() is the training method. It teaches the model by showing it examples (X_train) and their correct answers (y_train). The model learns patterns during this process.

questions.question: If a model has 95% accuracy, what does this mean?
options: The model is 5% broken
The model correctly predicts 95 out of 100 test cases on average
Training took 95 seconds
The model is perfect and ready for production
questions.explanation: 95% accuracy means the model correctly predicts 95% of test cases. For example, if you have 100 test flowers, it correctly classifies about 95 of them. This is good but not perfect - 5% are still misclassified.

questions.question: In a binary classification problem, how many classes are being predicted?
options: 3 or more
It varies
questions.explanation: Binary classification has exactly 2 classes (e.g., spam/ham, yes/no, positive/negative). Multi-class classification has 3 or more classes (like Iris with 3 species).

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a classification problem?
options: Predicting house prices
Predicting if an email is spam or not spam
Predicting tomorrow's temperature
Predicting stock prices
questions.explanation: Email spam detection is classification (2 categories: spam or not spam). House prices, temperature, and stock prices are regression problems because they predict continuous numbers.

questions.question: You train a model on 120 samples and test on 30 samples. What is the train/test split ratio?
questions.explanation: 120 training + 30 testing = 150 total. 120/150 = 80% for training, 30/150 = 20% for testing. This is the standard 80/20 split used in the Iris project.

sessions.title: Evaluation Metrics & Confusion Matrix
sessions.description: Covers accuracy limitations, confusion matrix, precision, recall, F1-score

questions.question: What does a confusion matrix show?
options: How confused the data scientist is
Actual vs predicted classifications for all test samples
Only the incorrect predictions
The training time for each algorithm
questions.explanation: A confusion matrix is a table showing actual classes (rows) vs predicted classes (columns). It reveals not just how many mistakes, but specifically which classes get confused with each other.

questions.question: In a confusion matrix, where do correct predictions appear?
options: In the off-diagonal cells
In the diagonal from top-left to bottom-right
In the bottom row only
They don't appear in the confusion matrix
questions.explanation: Correct predictions appear on the main diagonal (top-left to bottom-right). For example, if actual=setosa and predicted=setosa, it goes in the [setosa, setosa] cell on the diagonal. Off-diagonal cells show misclassifications.

questions.question: What is precision in classification?
options: Of all positive predictions, how many were actually positive?
Of all actual positives, how many did we find?
The overall accuracy of the model
The speed at which the model makes predictions
questions.explanation: Precision = True Positives / (True Positives + False Positives). It answers: 'When my model says positive, how often is it right?' High precision means few false alarms.

questions.question: What is recall in classification?
options: Of all positive predictions, how many were actually positive?
Of all actual positives, how many did we find?
The ability to remember training data
The same as accuracy
questions.explanation: Recall = True Positives / (True Positives + False Negatives). It answers: 'Of all the actual positives, how many did I catch?' High recall means few missed cases.

questions.question: For a cancer detection model, which metric is more critical?
options: Precision - minimize false alarms
Recall - catch all actual cancer cases
Accuracy - overall correctness
F1-score - balanced approach
questions.explanation: In medical diagnosis, recall is critical because missing a cancer case (false negative) is worse than a false alarm (false positive). We want to catch ALL actual cancer cases, even if it means some false positives that can be ruled out with further testing.

questions.question: What is the F1-score?
options: The first feature in a dataset
The harmonic mean of precision and recall
The same as accuracy
The speed of the algorithm
questions.explanation: F1-score = 2 * (Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall). It's the harmonic mean, which balances precision and recall. It's useful when you need both low false positives AND low false negatives.

questions.question: Why might accuracy be misleading for imbalanced datasets?
options: Accuracy always gives wrong results
A model predicting only the majority class can still have high accuracy
Accuracy cannot be calculated for imbalanced data
Imbalanced data makes training slower
questions.explanation: If 95% of emails are ham and 5% spam, a model that always predicts 'ham' gets 95% accuracy while being completely useless at detecting spam. This is why precision, recall, and F1 are important for imbalanced data.

questions.question: You have a confusion matrix for binary classification: [[90, 10], [5, 95]]. What is the accuracy?
questions.explanation: Accuracy = (True Positives + True Negatives) / Total = (90 + 95) / (90 + 10 + 5 + 95) = 185/200 = 92.5%. The diagonal elements (90, 95) are correct predictions, off-diagonal (10, 5) are errors.

sessions.title: Multiple Classification Algorithms
sessions.description: Covers Decision Trees, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, KNN, SVM, and algorithm comparison

questions.question: How does a Decision Tree make predictions?
options: By following a series of if-then rules based on features
By calculating the average of all training data
By using complex mathematical equations
By randomly selecting a class
questions.explanation: Decision Trees work like a flowchart of yes/no questions. For example: 'Is petal length < 2.5cm? If yes, predict setosa. If no, is petal width < 1.7cm?' Each branch is an if-then rule learned from training data.

questions.question: What does K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) do to classify a new sample?
options: Finds the K closest training samples and takes a majority vote
Creates a tree of decisions
Calculates probabilities using logistic function
Generates random predictions
questions.explanation: KNN finds the K nearest training samples (using distance measures) and predicts based on majority vote. For example, if K=5 and 4 neighbors are 'spam' and 1 is 'ham', it predicts 'spam'.

questions.question: What is an ensemble method in machine learning?
options: Using a single powerful algorithm
Combining multiple models to make better predictions
Training on the entire dataset at once
A type of neural network
questions.explanation: Ensemble methods combine multiple models (often weak learners) to create a stronger predictor. Random Forest is an ensemble of many Decision Trees. Wisdom of the crowd!

questions.question: How does Random Forest differ from a single Decision Tree?
options: Random Forest uses one very large tree
Random Forest trains multiple trees on random subsets and averages results
Random Forest is faster but less accurate
They are exactly the same
questions.explanation: Random Forest creates many Decision Trees, each trained on a random subset of data and features. Final prediction is based on majority vote (classification) or average (regression). This reduces overfitting and improves accuracy.

questions.question: Despite its name, Logistic Regression is used for:
options: Predicting continuous values
Classification problems
Clustering data
Dimensionality reduction
questions.explanation: Logistic Regression is a classification algorithm (confusing name!). It uses a logistic/sigmoid function to output probabilities between 0 and 1, which are then converted to class predictions. It's widely used for binary classification.

questions.question: Why might different algorithms give different accuracy scores on the same dataset?
options: Some algorithms are broken
Different algorithms make different assumptions and learn different patterns
It's due to random errors only
All algorithms should give the same accuracy
questions.explanation: Each algorithm has different assumptions and learning strategies. Decision Trees find rule boundaries, KNN uses distance similarity, Logistic Regression finds linear decision boundaries. Some algorithms work better for certain types of patterns in data.

questions.question: What is the 'K' hyperparameter in K-Nearest Neighbors?
options: The number of features to use
The number of training samples
The number of neighbors to consider for voting
The number of classes to predict
questions.explanation: K is the number of nearest neighbors to look at when making a prediction. K=1 means use only the closest neighbor. K=5 means find 5 closest neighbors and take majority vote. Higher K means smoother decision boundaries but less sensitivity to local patterns.

questions.question: You test 4 algorithms and get accuracies: DT=93%, LR=96%, RF=95%, KNN=97%. Which should you choose?
options: Always choose the highest accuracy (KNN)
Always choose the fastest algorithm
Consider accuracy, speed, interpretability, and production requirements
Choose the one with the shortest name
questions.explanation: While KNN has highest accuracy, consider: (1) Is 97% vs 96% meaningful? (2) Speed: KNN is slow on large datasets (3) Interpretability: Decision Trees are most explainable (4) Production needs: Some models deploy better. Often, a slightly less accurate but faster/simpler model is preferred.

sessions.title: Text Classification & Spam Detection
sessions.description: Covers text preprocessing, vectorization, Bag of Words, TF-IDF, and spam detection

questions.question: Why do we need to convert text to numbers for machine learning?
options: To make the text easier to read
Because ML algorithms work with numerical inputs, not raw text
To reduce the size of the text
Text doesn't need to be converted
questions.explanation: Machine learning algorithms perform mathematical operations on numbers. Raw text like 'free money' can't be multiplied or compared mathematically. We must convert text to numerical features (vectors) that capture meaning.

questions.question: What does text preprocessing typically include?
options: Only removing punctuation
Converting to lowercase, removing punctuation, removing stop words, tokenization
Making the text longer
Translating to different languages
questions.explanation: Text preprocessing cleans text for ML: lowercase (so 'Free' and 'free' are same), remove punctuation, remove common words (the, a, is), split into tokens (words). This creates consistent, meaningful features.

questions.question: What is the 'Bag of Words' model?
options: A model that predicts word meanings
Converting text to vectors based on word counts, ignoring order
A storage container for vocabulary
A type of deep learning model
questions.explanation: Bag of Words treats text as a collection (bag) of words without considering order. Each document becomes a vector of word counts. Example: 'free money' and 'money free' have the same vector. Simple but effective for many tasks.

questions.question: How does TF-IDF differ from simple word counts?
options: TF-IDF weights words by how unique they are to a document
TF-IDF only counts words once
TF-IDF is the same as word counts
TF-IDF removes all common words
questions.explanation: TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) gives higher weight to words that appear frequently in a document but rarely across all documents. A word appearing in every document (like 'the') gets low weight. Unique words (like 'viagra' in spam) get high weight.

questions.question: Which algorithm is commonly used for spam detection and why?
options: K-Nearest Neighbors, because it's the fastest
Naive Bayes, because it works well with text and probability
Linear Regression, because it's simple
Random Forest, because it has 'forest' in the name
questions.explanation: Naive Bayes is popular for text classification because: (1) It works well with high-dimensional text data (2) It calculates probability of spam given certain words (3) It's fast to train and predict (4) Despite 'naive' independence assumption, it works remarkably well for text.

questions.question: What is the main challenge with imbalanced spam datasets?
options: There's too much data to process
Models may achieve high accuracy by predicting only the majority class
Imbalanced data cannot be used for training
It makes training slower
questions.explanation: If 95% of emails are ham, a model predicting everything as 'ham' gets 95% accuracy while being useless at detecting spam. Solutions include: using precision/recall instead of accuracy, oversampling minority class, undersampling majority class, or using class weights.

questions.question: What does the vocabulary size represent in text classification?
options: The number of documents in the dataset
The total number of unique words across all documents
The number of features in the original data
The accuracy of the model
questions.explanation: Vocabulary is the set of unique words found in the training data. If your spam dataset contains 10,000 unique words total, your feature vectors will have 10,000 dimensions (one for each word's count/TF-IDF score).

questions.question: You build a spam detector with 98% accuracy, 90% precision, and 60% recall. What does this mean?
options: The model is perfect and ready for production
The model is good overall but misses 40% of actual spam emails
The model only works 60% of the time
The model is completely broken
questions.explanation: 98% accuracy looks great, but 60% recall means you're only catching 60% of actual spam - missing 40%! The 90% precision means when it says 'spam', it's usually right. This model might let too much spam through. For spam detection, you might want to tune for higher recall.

sessions.title: Real-World Classification: Customer Churn & Feature Engineering
sessions.description: Covers business applications, feature engineering, handling categorical data, class imbalance

questions.question: What is customer churn?
options: When customers buy more products
When customers leave/cancel their service
When customers refer friends
When customers change their address
questions.explanation: Churn means customers leaving or canceling service. Predicting churn is valuable for businesses because: (1) It's cheaper to retain customers than acquire new ones (2) You can intervene with offers/support before they leave (3) You can identify at-risk customer segments.

questions.question: What is feature engineering?
options: Creating new features from existing data to improve model performance
Removing all features from the dataset
Engineering the model architecture
The same as data collection
questions.explanation: Feature engineering creates new, more informative features from raw data. Examples: (1) From date → extract day_of_week, month, is_weekend (2) From first_name, last_name → create full_name (3) From total_charges, months → create avg_monthly_charge. Good features can dramatically improve model performance.

questions.question: How do you handle categorical variables (like 'gender': Male/Female) in ML?
options: Remove them from the dataset
Leave them as text strings
Convert to numbers using Label Encoding or One-Hot Encoding
Use them only for visualization
questions.explanation: ML needs numbers. For categorical variables: (1) Label Encoding: Male=0, Female=1 (good for ordinal data) (2) One-Hot Encoding: Creates binary columns Male_0, Male_1, Female_0, Female_1 (better for nominal data with no order). One-hot avoids implying false ordering.

questions.question: What is One-Hot Encoding?
options: Increasing the temperature of the dataset
Converting categorical variables into binary columns (0s and 1s)
Selecting only one feature for training
A type of neural network
questions.explanation: One-hot encoding converts categories to binary columns. Example: Color [Red, Blue, Green] becomes: is_Red [1,0,0], is_Blue [0,1,0], is_Green [0,0,1]. Each sample has exactly one '1' (hot) and others are '0'. This prevents ML from treating categories as having numerical order.

questions.question: For a churn prediction model, why might you prioritize recall over precision?
options: Precision is never useful
Because missing at-risk customers (false negatives) is costly
Recall is always better than precision
Because it makes the model train faster
questions.explanation: In churn prediction, high recall means you catch most customers who will leave, even if you get some false alarms (predicting churn when they won't leave). Missing a customer who's about to leave (false negative) means losing their revenue. A false alarm just means sending an unnecessary retention offer - less costly.

questions.question: What is SMOTE in the context of imbalanced datasets?
options: A type of neural network
A technique to create synthetic samples of the minority class
A method to remove outliers
A visualization tool
questions.explanation: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) creates artificial samples of the minority class by interpolating between existing minority samples. If only 5% of customers churn, SMOTE generates synthetic churned customers to balance the dataset, helping the model learn better.

questions.question: Which feature would be most useful for predicting customer churn?
options: Customer ID number
Number of support tickets in last month
Customer's favorite color
The day they first signed up (exact date)
questions.explanation: Support tickets indicate customer dissatisfaction, a strong churn signal. Customer ID is arbitrary and has no predictive value. Exact signup date is too specific (but tenure/months as customer would be useful). Favorite color is unrelated to churn. Good features have logical connection to the target.

questions.question: You have a churn dataset with 20 features. After feature engineering, you have 50 features. What should you consider?
options: More features always mean better models
Too many features can lead to overfitting and slower training
You should always use all available features
Feature count doesn't matter
questions.explanation: More features aren't always better. Risks: (1) Overfitting - model memorizes noise (2) Curse of dimensionality - need more data (3) Slower training/prediction (4) Some features may be irrelevant. Use feature selection, feature importance analysis, or dimensionality reduction to keep only useful features.

sessions.title: Advanced Topics: Medical Diagnosis, Model Interpretation & Production
sessions.description: Covers healthcare ML, ROC/AUC, feature importance, ethics, and production considerations

questions.question: In medical diagnosis ML, why is recall often more important than precision?
options: Precision is too complicated to calculate
Missing a disease (false negative) can be life-threatening
Recall is easier to optimize
Medical datasets are always balanced
questions.explanation: In healthcare, false negatives (missing a disease) can delay treatment and harm patients. False positives (diagnosing disease when absent) lead to additional testing but are less dangerous. We prioritize catching all actual cases (high recall) even if it means some false alarms.

questions.question: What does the ROC curve show?
options: Training time vs accuracy
True Positive Rate vs False Positive Rate at different thresholds
Precision vs Recall
Feature importance
questions.explanation: ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve plots True Positive Rate (recall) on y-axis vs False Positive Rate on x-axis, as you vary the classification threshold. It shows the tradeoff between catching positives and avoiding false alarms. Area under curve (AUC) measures overall performance.

questions.question: What does an AUC (Area Under ROC Curve) of 0.5 indicate?
options: Perfect model
Model performs no better than random guessing
Model is 50% accurate
Model needs more training data
questions.explanation: AUC ranges from 0 to 1. AUC=0.5 means the ROC curve is a diagonal line - the model is no better than randomly guessing. AUC=1.0 is perfect. AUC=0.7-0.8 is acceptable, 0.8-0.9 is good, >0.9 is excellent. It's threshold-independent, making it useful for comparison.

questions.question: What is feature importance in machine learning?
options: How important the dataset is
Which features contribute most to the model's predictions
The order features appear in the dataset
How many features the model uses
questions.explanation: Feature importance ranks features by how much they contribute to predictions. Decision Trees and Random Forests provide this automatically. High importance features are key drivers. This helps: (1) Understand the model (2) Identify key factors (3) Reduce features (4) Communicate insights to stakeholders.

questions.question: Why is model interpretability important in healthcare applications?
options: To make models train faster
Doctors need to understand why the model made a prediction before trusting it
It's not important - only accuracy matters
To reduce the dataset size
questions.explanation: In healthcare, doctors are legally and ethically responsible for decisions. They need to understand WHY a model predicts disease to: (1) Trust the prediction (2) Explain to patients (3) Combine with clinical judgment (4) Identify errors. Black-box models, even if accurate, may not be acceptable.

questions.question: What is cross-validation?
options: Validating data is correct
Training and evaluating a model on multiple different train/test splits
Testing on training data
Asking someone else to review your code
questions.explanation: Cross-validation (e.g., 5-fold CV) splits data into K parts. Train on K-1 parts, test on 1 part. Repeat K times, rotating which part is test. Average the K results. This gives more reliable performance estimate than single train/test split and uses all data for both training and testing.

questions.question: Before deploying a classification model to production, you should:
options: Only check that it runs without errors
Check accuracy on test set and deploy immediately
Evaluate on test set, validate on new data, monitor performance, plan for updates
Deploy and hope for the best
questions.explanation: Production deployment requires: (1) Test set evaluation (2) Validation on fresh data not seen during development (3) Consider edge cases (4) Monitor performance over time (data drift) (5) Plan for retraining (6) Error handling (7) Logging predictions (8) A/B testing if possible. Models degrade over time as patterns change.

questions.question: You notice your deployed model's accuracy dropped from 92% to 78% over 6 months. What likely happened?
options: The model deleted itself
Data drift - the real-world data distribution changed over time
The model got tired
This is impossible - models don't change
questions.explanation: Data drift occurs when the real-world data distribution changes from training data. Examples: customer behavior changes, new spam tactics emerge, economic conditions shift. Solutions: (1) Monitor performance metrics (2) Retrain periodically with fresh data (3) Set up alerts for accuracy drops (4) Version models and data.
